text: Welcome to This is my Architecture. Today I have here Carlos from Majid El Futame. Hi Carlos.
Hi Cameron. Hi. Can you tell me a little bit more about Majid El Futame?
Yeah. Majid El Futame is a multi-veilion company. We are in 14 countries and we have
business like retail, real estate, we have malls and we have patient entertainment.
I see that we have a very interesting architecture here today. Tell me what
problem you are trying to solve. So the three pillars we want to solve is how to
collect the data, how to protect the data, and how to connect the data. Okay so you
have data from various sources and you want to get value out of that, extract
value out of it. Exactly. So we call it the grid. So we start all of the
infrastructure is defined with our DevOps team in code so we can just spin the
architecture or the infrastructure from them and then you get your frontend and
you connect to our MetaManager. Okay and what is the MetaManager? The
manager is the brains of the grid and the main goal is to abstract you from the
complexity of all this technology you need to move this data. And I see that
you're running that on EKS. Yeah it's EKS microservice architecture. So that's
collecting the data from the various sources. You have Elasticsearch there as
well. Yes. What's happening there? So first you need to understand which data you
have. So you need to discover which tables, schemas, APIs you have. Right so we get all
of this data into MetaManager. We'll discover and we'll store this meta information in our
MetaStore which is a combination of document and graph database. Okay so now
you have indexed the data. What's next? So first step is collect as we mentioned
before. Right so we need to get all of this data into our centralized data lake so
we can get all the data together and we can do insights etc. Right so all of this data
are in different networks, different countries, different technology. So we
use a combination of Kafka and EMR to bring this data into our data lake. Okay so
you're taking data from into Kafka and streaming them into S3 and EMR. What kind
of scale are we talking about? Yeah so in Kafka we are talking about 600 topics,
250 gigabytes and in average we have seven days attention period. So all of the data is stored in S3.
Okay and what kind of scale do we have in S3? Yeah so in S3 we have around 60 terabytes today. Sorry for my writing.
No problem. Great amount of data there. All right so now you have collected the data in the data lake.
What's next? How do you consume this? Yeah so first let me just explain. So we
asked the data coming in and out of data lake. Right?
So this data we need to protect the data. Right? So our metamani also will apply all of these data

So the data is sent to these data stores. We can publish or consume using APIs or OLTPs if we need.
Okay and this is your data analyst and data scientist are going to consume this data.
Exactly. So this is a traditional analytics we have in this company. You have your data analyst and your data science.
They need to consume the data. They need to run models. They need to create the ask words etc.
Great. So now you have consumed the data. I collected the data. How do we connect to users?
That's a very good point. Data comes from different sources and applications because we also consume from APIs
and then we bring this data back to our business. So we are able to connect back.
One of the key components is the enterprise streaming platform that we created across all the companies in
Mav. This is something that we bring to Mav. So basically I go to watch a movie at Vox Cinema and then I go to Carrefour
to do shopping. What kind of experience am I getting? That's a very very good point. Mind you come to
one of our iconic malls, Mall of Emirates. You come with your car. You park. So we get your plate number.
We know where you park. You enter inside the mall. You connect to the wi-fi. We know how you move inside
the mall. Now you are doing your groceries in Carrefour and you use our loyalty application. So we know that
you've come to the cinemas before. We know you like cinema. So we can provide for you a push notification
and we can offer to you a free back service. So we'll take your groceries, bring to your car and then you can come to the movies with a free popcorn pouch.
I'll always love free popcorn. Great. So I know you have made this. There's a lot of complexity in our
architecture and we've really simplified it. What is one thing that you're really proud of from your team
that's delivered this architecture? Yeah. So we provide this DevOps approach to the company and we are able to
spin up and spin down the infrastructure as we need. So for example, in our IT test, we create all
the infrastructure. We run the test and then we destroy the infrastructure and we built this in
six months. We put our first MVP in production. Wow. And for a non-technology company spinning it up
and spinning down infrastructure as code. Yeah. That's great. So what kind of a business benefit has
your company realized after this data? Yeah. So one of the first things that we provided is a 360 view of
our customers. So before you were a customer in Vox and a customer in Carrefour and we couldn't relate
together. Now we know you are the same customer. So we can do cross campaigns, for example, for
marketing. So we can connect Carrefour and Vox with our marketing in holding and we can do these
campaigns like the push notification I explained. That's good. Carlos, thank you for sharing your work
